sentences.text: After the Russian and German war started, because we had one war in '39, and the second war just started between Russia and Germany, which started in [NON-ENGLISH], 1941.

So, uh, since, uh, things got hard in [NON-ENGLISH], which was, uh, getting practically impossible to obtain food and obtain food that you could eat because the food got so horrible that even you were hungry, you know, you couldn't swallow it.
So my brother and I decided to leave that place because, as far we could hear the news, that the Germans are getting closer and that was not where we wanted to be-- closer to the Germans.

[CLEARS THROAT] In the meantime, uh, the-- what do you call it in English?-- the commissioner, from, the military commissioner in that little town, he met my brother and I and he said, why don't you induct, he said, to the Polish Army?
There was a Polish army already created in Russia.
And, uh, so we were thinking maybe it's a good idea.
But in the meantime, we got to-- some word that Russia is having an amnesty for Polish people that were in-- in camps, in labor camps, in Russia.
They're releasing them.
And this is the element they're going to induct in that Polish army that's being created in Russia.
And they should go to the Middle East, fight the Germans over Afghanistan, and through Iran.
Yeah.
So, uh, but we had to go to-- to Central Asia.
This is where the-- the headquarters was for-- to induct Polish citizens, you know, to gather them and-- and send them overseas.
So my brother and I decided we're going to go.
And my brother left before I did, because he thought, oh, you're going to meet me at a certain place, you know, where we're going to meet.
So, fine.
And I still had to work.
But in the meantime, [CLEARS THROAT] they sent me-- what do you call it?-- a letter, you know, that I should go to Russian Army.
So I couldn't say no.
So about two days later, I had to come, you know, to the induction place and say, I'm here to register.
I'm here.
And everything was formal and fine.
And I was waiting from morning until late in the afternoon.
And everybody-- they created groups, you know, like commanders.
And they took them-- I don't know where they took them, whether to the railroad station or where to take them to their desti-- destination.
At the end, I was left alone-- all alone in that big place.
But, uh, there was a-- a guard with a-- with a gun, you know, at the door, so you couldn't go no place.
So I-- I talked to that guard.
I said, listen, everybody left.
They called everybody's name, and I'm sitting here, waiting to be called and nobody calls me.
He said, let me ask the commissioner.
So he went to the commissioner.
He came back in about 10 minutes.
He said, where you from?
I said from Poland.
Oh, you're not Russian.
You're from Poland.
Then you can go home.
So I went home.
But then the only thing was left really to go into a Polish Army.
And at work, I told them-- I went to the office and I told them that I had to go.
I have to go to the Polish Army.
So they gave me-- [CLEARS THROAT] you see, everybody that is-- that leaves work, a job, has to have a booklet, you know, where it says, what was the reason.
Because during the war, you couldn't just go away and go in another place and work.
You had to have a permission.
And the permission was in the little booklet.
The reason why I'm going away for work, why I'm-- I'm left to go.
So they wrote down because I wanted to go to the Polish Army.
This is the only way I could get a ticket on the railroad.
Otherwise, you're locked in, you know, you couldn't go no place.
So I got a ticket and I-- I boarded the train and I went all on my own.
And I know-- I knew where to go.
And I knew where I'm going to meet my brother.
What was his name again?
Henry, my brother Henry.
So it was-- it was rough, you know, because to trains are all overfilled, crowded.
You know?
And we traveled about five days and five nights.
In-- a lot of times I was just-- there was no place to sit down, to stand up.
And if I got a place, you know, I was afraid to go up, stand up and go to the bathroom because I-- I couldn't get my place back.
So finally, when I came to my destination, I didn't feel my-- my flesh, here.
It was all numb, just from sitting down because standing up was no pleasure, and sitting down, you know, while you were sitting down, you didn't know.
But when I stand up, you know, I stood up and I felt, you know, that it was not a [? picnic ?] to-- to sit for such a long time.
And [CLEARS THROAT] when I came to my brother, that was a little town, Guzar, that was close to the Afghanistan border.
So my brother was working there, [COUGHS] and they didn't take him to the-- [COUGHS] to the Polish Army, because the Polish Army didn't take any Jews.
It was forbidden, not officially-- I mean, not legally, but officially.
[COUGHS] [DRINKS] So they put you on a medical, uh, board, you know, the medical, uh, examination, and they give you a category that you are unable to-- to be a soldier in the Army.
[CLEARS THROAT]
Because-- it wasn't because.
It was because, but not written down.
[COUGHS] So what is there to do?
So I went to my brother and, eh, uh, there was no place to work over there, so I went to [NON ENGLISH].
That was still in '41.
And times are very bad-- '41, in particular, was very bad, when it started, you know, everything.
So.
And I went to [NON ENGLISH] and they were raising, uh, sheep, you know, for fur to-- Persian lamb, Persian lamb.
So I worked over there as electrician.
I worked, uh, and, uh--
I met with Jews.
Uh, There was a guy from Romania, a Jew.
He was a mechanic.
And, uh--
No, no, no, no.
In Russia, there was one thing-- you know, they didn't look for Jews, and Jews didn't look for Jews.
They look for people.
You know?
This is the way it should be-- people, you know?
There's good Jews.
There are bad Jews.
They're good Gentiles, bad Gentiles.
You know, if you got-- meet somebody good, you want to-- he wants to be a friend of yours, you want to be a friend of him, so you-- so you associate.
Oh, no.
Oh, no.
First of all, there was laws, certain laws, against discrimination, which the-- [COUGHS] the government was passing laws.
So discrimination was a bad word.
It was very bad to-- to call somebody, uh, you know, a dirty Jew, a dumb Polack, you know, like here.
You didn't have those things, you know?
Unless if somebody said that, you know, which it happened to me.
A boy told me 'dirty Jew,' so I went to my boss.
He took that to his office and he scolded him pretty good and so he remembered.
You know, he said, 'next time, you know, I'm going to go to the police and you're going to wind up, you know, where you belong, if you don't behave like a human being.'
So that was really encouraging.
And it was nice, you know, to be where you can feel-- you know?
And I didn't feel, as a Jew, you know, that I'm mistreated, or-- by a boss, or whatever.
You know?
It was-- it was [INAUDIBLE].
If you're a good worker, they appreciate it, and they really-- if you were worth promoting, they give you a promotion.
You know, it was-- it was really nice.
But you didn't have no freedom.
If you said something, like my friends from Warsaw, [CLEARS THROAT] they came to-- to eat in that restaurant, the food, and they said the food is bad.
Oh, it's so horrible.
So that was the talk against the government, because everything was owned by the government.
You see, if the chair you did-- wasn't comfortable, you know, the chair was wobbly, you said something about the chair, you talk against the government.
And little by little, it add up, and they send you away to a camp, to a labor camp, to slave labor camps.
You know?
And so you talk against the government, so you're against, uh-- against Communism.
You were not even, uh, politically inclined, you know, but you were against-- against Communism.
And that's all.
So I knew to watch out, you know, and not to talk against the government, because it wouldn't help you if you do.
They had-- they had people that were, uh-- they were, uh-- how do you say it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The police took them in, especially to spy on other citizens, you know, even in their own family.
You know?
So if somebody asks you questions, you know, you knew-- oh, like some guys told me.
Oh, I heard in Poland it was so good, you know, and it was-- life was a lot better than here.
I said, are you kidding?
What do you mean life is better?
It was a lot worse.
You don't know how good you have it here.
[INAUDIBLE].
So he said, oh, I know there's-- in the-- in the stores, there's a lot of, uh, stuff, a lot of food, a lot of that.
I said, because people, uh, don't have jobs.
They don't make any money, so they can't buy the-- the-- the stuff, so it stays in the-- in the stores.
Nobody buys it.
Nobody can afford it.
Here, if you got a little money, you run to the store and the shelves are empty.
So that saved me a lot of trouble.
[LAUGHS] Did you know anybody who was sent to a slave labor camp?
Oh yeah, I told you.
Some people that we're [INAUDIBLE] together.
[INTERPOSING VOICES]
Four friends, two brothers, and two friends from Warsaw.
They send them away.
Nobody knew.
Maybe they came out of there, you know, because there was amnesty later on.
But they were-- they were gone.
You know?
My own cousin, too.
Huh?
Bosh-- Boris.
Boris?
He was with us too.
And, uh-- I-- I have him in a picture someplace.
And, uh, he was talking too.
He was going out with girls, so he was bragging a little bit, too.
You know?
So finally they put him away.
But he was lucky that, uh, that they let him go.
He was a nice-- he had a personality, so he-- he was a likeable person.
So they let him go after four or five months, to let him go and he came home.
But he told us, you know, how-- how tough it is over there.
In the winter time, you know, it's-- it's 50 degrees below outside.
And you're roasting inside because the cell is overcrowded.
The twice or more than twice of people, you know, that should be occ-- occupying that cell.
Huh?
It was tough.
Because if you were in plain-- plain shoes, you know, you freeze your-- your-- your feet.
You freeze your butt, [LAUGHS] you know?
And I was ashamed to put on-- they had those felt-- felt boots.
You know?
And they didn't look good to me.
[LAUGHS] So-- so they wanted to give it to me.
I didn't have to buy it from the factory where I work, you know?
They wanted to give them to me.
I didn't want them.
I was ashame-- no, I was a stupid teenager.
[LAUGHS] I wanted to look good.
So it was really cold.
One morning, I went to work, you know, I froze my cheek and-- and-- this ear.
I still have trouble with that ear.
And it was really cold.
It was no joke.
Just to walk from the-- from the quarters where I lived, you know, to the factory, it was like from here to the next street.
You know, it's-- it was [INAUDIBLE].
But, as I say, when you're young, you know, things are different than when you get older.
He became a, uh, a car mechanic.
Yeah, yeah.
We were together there, and then we went from there, from Guzar, where the Polish Army was organize-- organizing.

And we couldn't get into the army, so in order to leave, so we had to go and look for other things, you know, where we can settle down for the time being steady, because we never thought we would be able to get out from Russia.
You know?
So we went to [NON-ENGLISH], a city [NON-ENGLISH].
And over there, my brother went to work as a mechanic in the-- in the truck, uh-- how do you call it?
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Oh, there were about 60 trucks, you know, a fleet from 60 trucks.
You know, they were holding stuff.
And I went to work in a sugar factory as electrician.
And, uh, over there, we-- we worked, and it was-- it was not easy.
Like in the weekends, you know, in the winter time, they come-- you had to go unload trucks, you know, with merchandise that come, you know, heavy stuff.
You know?
And you work for nothing, because it's for the government.
They were short-handed because of the war, you know, so you have to go and help out, you know, to [INAUDIBLE].
And we did it.
Everybody did it, you know, because it was just a way of life.
You know? ?] What were living conditions like there?
Living conditions-- very primitive.
Very primitive.
I, uh-- I-- I contracted certain health, uh, you know, misgivings, you know, which I'm still suffering, you know, like rheumatism and arthritis.
You know?
When you get older, you know, you don't get better, you get worse.
[LAUGHS]

Well, you get medical attention, but somehow, I didn't recognize it that it's any sickness, because while I was asleep, I was-- my whole body was hurt, you know, because there was no-- no-- [CLEARS THROAT].
The temperature in the room was cold.
There was no heat, you see?
So I got up in the morning.
I got dressed.
I had to go to work.
You know, I felt better, so I didn't care to go to-- to seek medical attention.
Uh.
I didn't want to give in.
So anyway, [CLEARS THROAT] from over there, you know, they started organizing again, the Polish people, you know, Polish citizens.
And, uh, and that had already a little flavor, you know, like we could sense that they're going to let us go back home, you know, because the Russians started already winning the war.
They were pushing the Germans day-by-day, you know, back and back.
That was past Stalingrad.
You know, so we were looking already forwards, you know, that someday, maybe, there's going to be a miracle.
And, uh, that committee, you know, the Polish committee, was supported by American organizations and Jewish organizations.
They were sending clothes.
They were sending, uh, food, you know, to help, and it was quite a big help.
You know?
You could smell America already over there.
[LAUGHS] You know?
It was really a great thing, a great job that America is doing the world over for needy people.
You know?
That was the first thing, you know, that I could feel, you know, that-- that somebody is up there, you know, helping.
[LAUGHS] So, little by little, you know, after [CLEARS THROAT] the war, you know, came closer to an end, you know, we felt a little more free, a little more the burden of war, you know.
And then the help that we got from America was also, you know, helped-- helped a lot, you know.
We could have some good clothes, some food.
You know?
It was-- it was quite a great thing.
And, uh, then they came to us, the Russian, uh-- from the KGB, from the militia.
And they said that we'll be able to go home, you know?
That there's people already started.
You know, and we have to register to-- to go home, and, uh-- it's going to be-- you know.
Because over there, they had already-- it looked like later that we found out that they had already Communist governments in Poland and Czechoslovakia.
See, so they didn't mind, you know, if you go home, because it's going to be easier on them to have foreigners in their [? midst. ?] You know?
And it's going to be better, you know, for Poland that we're going to go back and, you know, they need more people to work, or whatever.
So one day, you know, it happened.
We w-- we started going back, but still in cattle cars.
[LAUGHS] Maybe because-- Was the war over then?
Huh?
The war was over already.
I was in, uh, in Shymkent.
And that was, uh-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]] it's the southern-- southern, uh, state of Kazakhstan, southern part of Kazakhstan, Shymkent.
The-- the climate, as a matter of fact, is just like here, you know, exactly the same climate-- mild winters, you know, and-- so and we started already getting prepared to leave.
My brother and I.
And-- and, um, [CLEARS THROAT] I joined up with my wife's family, my future wife's family, because I lived, uh, next door [CLEARS THROAT] with them.
We were neighbors.
And I worked with her father.
I worked in the same place, you know, where they had that-- [CLEARS THROAT] that truck fleet, you know?
Yeah, I got away from-- from that, uh, sugar factory.
I didn't-- I didn't tell you about that.
You know?
[CLEARS THROAT] It came, a guy, and told a friend of ours that they need workers in-- in Shymkent.
So he was a-- a mechanic, that guy.
And he was a good friend of ours.
So I said, you know what?
I'm going to tell them, you know, maybe they need-- my brother was already a chauffeur, a chauffeur and  electrician, you know maybe you could come along.
And he did.
So it was legal and it was not legal, you know?
[LAUGHS] So we-- from their side, they needed us, so it was legal.
But from the side from the factory that I was was yes and no.
I didn't ask questions because sometimes you ask questions, you know, you know, you get disappointed.
So we-- they-- they provided a truck for us.
You know?
We left for Shymkent.
In Shymkent, uh, they treated us pretty good.
You know?
I was electrician.
I had my own, uh-- what do you call it?-- uh, place, you know, to work, just for electrical parts.
We-- we fixed the-- the [INAUDIBLE], you know?
And all the electrical thing and the-- the trucks.
You know?
Everything, you had to fix, because they didn't get any new stuff because of the war.
Because the war-- everybody was working for the purpose of the war.
So we had to just fix what we had.
You know?
So that's why that was a great need for people like-- like myself.
I-- the-- the-- batteries, you know, I had to make from two [INAUDIBLE] to make one, or from three [INAUDIBLE] I took a part here, a part there.
So to get those-- those machines, those trucks going.
You know?
So it was a need.
And so I-- we worked until, as I said, we-- it was time to leave and go back west.
[BACKGROUND NOISE] So by that time, they boarded us on, uh-- on, uh, you know-- how did I say, those cars-- freight cars, in the same way.
You know, we started moving back a little faster paced than when we came, because it was already more easier because the war zone was already gone.
You know?
But a lot of people were going back, you know, because Russia eva-- evacuated a lot of people, especially women and children, from the war zones.
So these places got crowded more and more.
So a lot of people started going back.
So when we came back to Poland, we came to the border, to the Polish border, so the [?
Poles ?] said, 'look, John, there's still so many Jews left.' Oh, that hit me so hard.
I could cry.

For all that they saw that the Jewish people went through, I figured-- because we were reading the papers-- you know, we didn't know that everybody, you know, was-- was-- almost everybody, you know, was, uh, gone, you know, to those selections, you know, and the concentration camps.
Everybody hoped, you know-- maybe somebody is left.
You know?
And here those people that they saw what went on, they still say something like that.
It was unbelievable, unbelievable.
So I-- I started planning already.
No, I'm not going-- even before the war, you know, when I was a little boy, I was dreaming about leaving that haven in Poland.
You know?
So here, I stand there and I said, well, this is time.
And then I heard-- I had-- I had a neighbor before the war, where we lived.
And he went through the war, the First World War.
So he said after the First World War, you could go every place, because the-- all the borders were open.
You didn't know no visas.
You didn't need no visas, nothing.
I said, oh, I hope now is going to be [LAUGH] so-- something like that.
So.
But in the meantime, you know, you have to go through everything, you know.
So we took us-- I think we'll stop this tape again.
Yeah.
